skill.name: agent-framework
skill.description: Scaffold Python agents and multi-agent workflows with Microsoft Agent Framework SDK, Azure AI Foundry integration, HTTP server mode, and VS Code debugging support.
skill.summary: Build Microsoft Agent Framework agents, workflows, server wrappers, and local debug setup.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and blocker patterns, including a critical combined heuristic. Review found the highest-severity matches are mostly Markdown examples, SDK role constants, localhost debug settings, and package install guidance. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because generated projects can run tools and write local configuration.

medium_findings.title: External Tool Execution in Generated Projects
medium_findings.description: The skill can scaffold MCP and debug patterns that run external tools, including an npx-based Playwright MCP server and Python debug commands. This is legitimate for agent development, but it creates supply-chain and local execution risk if users run generated projects without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly configure commands that a generated project may execute. The intent is normal developer tooling, so confidence is high for operational risk but not malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Environment File Creation and Override Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to always create or update a .env file with Foundry project settings. It also recommends load_dotenv override behavior. The shown values are project configuration rather than secrets. Automatic environment-file edits can affect deployed behavior and expose sensitive local configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of .env writing guidance and dotenv override guidance. The examples do not show credential exfiltration, so the concern is configuration handling rather than confirmed secret theft.

low_findings.title: Local Debug Server Commands Are Benign but Powerful
low_findings.description: The debug setup includes shell tasks that start local HTTP and debugpy listeners on localhost ports. The static hardcoded IP and command findings are mostly expected for VS Code debugging, but users should confirm ports and commands before running generated tasks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and addresses are visible and bound to local development usage. They are not evidence of remote exfiltration, but they do start local services.

low_findings.title: Official and Local Network References
low_findings.description: Network findings point to Microsoft Learn MCP, placeholder Foundry endpoints, and localhost debug ports. No evidence found of unknown external collection endpoints or suspicious data transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited network targets are official documentation, placeholders, or loopback addresses. This strongly supports a false positive for malicious networking.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and role-token findings are not confirmed in context. Cited lines contain Markdown metadata, SDK examples, role enum usage, version pinning, or agent name validation guidance rather than cryptographic code, reconnaissance, or prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no matching dangerous operation at these cited locations. The patterns appear to be scanner confusion from Markdown text and SDK terminology.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated code or configuration may include package installation, npx MCP execution, agentdev, and debugpy commands. These commands should be reviewed before execution in user workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command templates are explicit and tied to normal setup and debugging. They are dangerous only if generated blindly or run in an untrusted workspace.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Configuration File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs assistants to generate .env, requirements.txt, .vscode configuration, and README files. This is expected scaffolding behavior, but it changes project files and can affect runtime configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file-write behavior is directly described in the skill workflow. It is legitimate for scaffolding, with moderate risk from unintended project modification.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable patterns for Python agents, workflows, HTTP server mode, and local debugging. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Microsoft Agent Framework scaffolding with focused reference examples.
actual_capabilities: Scaffolds Python agents using Microsoft Agent Framework SDK patterns.
Builds single-agent and multi-agent workflow examples with WorkflowBuilder.
Adds HTTP server mode using Azure AI Agent Server SDK guidance.
Creates VS Code debug tasks and launch settings for Agent Inspector workflows.
Shows MCP tool integration patterns for Playwright and Microsoft Learn.
Documents dependency pins and common Agent Framework setup errors.
limitations: Does not deploy agents to Azure or Microsoft Foundry.
Does not evaluate agent quality or safety after creation.
Requires users to provide valid Foundry endpoints and model deployments.
Uses beta SDK version pins that may need updates as Microsoft releases change.

use_cases.title: Create a New Agent Project
use_cases.description: Generate a Python agent scaffold with dependencies, environment placeholders, and a runnable entry point.

use_cases.title: Prototype Multi-Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Build workflow patterns where agents pass messages through typed executors and controlled edges.

use_cases.title: Prepare Local Debugging
use_cases.description: Add VS Code tasks and launch settings for HTTP server mode, debugpy, and Agent Inspector.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single Python agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a basic Microsoft Agent Framework Python agent for my project. Use placeholders for Foundry endpoint and model deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Add HTTP Server Mode
prompt_templates.scenario: Expose an existing agent through the server pattern.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update my existing Agent Framework agent to run as an HTTP server. Add the required package pins and explain the changed entry point.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Two-Agent Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a workflow with two collaborating agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a two-agent Microsoft Agent Framework workflow with a writer and reviewer pattern. Include streaming output handling and typed executor handlers.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Debugging and MCP Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup with local debugging and optional MCP tools.
prompt_templates.prompt: Add VS Code debug configuration and optional MCP tool setup to my Agent Framework project. Review each external command before adding it.

output_examples.input: Create a simple Foundry-backed agent with local configuration placeholders.
output_examples.output: A project plan with Python dependencies, environment keys, an agent entry point, and setup notes for the selected model deployment.

output_examples.input: Add debugging support for my workflow agent.
output_examples.output: VS Code task and launch guidance that starts the local HTTP server, attaches debugpy, and opens Agent Inspector.

output_examples.input: Convert my single agent into a workflow with review steps.
output_examples.output: A multi-agent workflow design with executor responsibilities, message flow, stop conditions, and verification steps.

best_practices: Review generated package commands before running them in a workspace.
Keep Foundry endpoints and deployment names in local environment configuration.
Pin SDK versions until the project is tested against newer Microsoft releases.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for Azure deployment tasks.
Do not commit generated .env files or local debug settings without review.
Do not enable MCP tools that run external commands without user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates Python Agent Framework scaffolds, workflow patterns, HTTP server guidance, and local debugging configuration.

faq.question: Can it deploy agents to Azure?
faq.answer: No. It prepares local agent projects but does not perform deployment.

faq.question: Does it require Azure AI Foundry?
faq.answer: Most Foundry examples require a project endpoint, a deployed model name, and Azure authentication.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: Generated projects may install packages, run external tools, start local servers, and write environment files.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include evaluation guidance?
faq.answer: No. Agent evaluation is outside this skill and should use a dedicated evaluation workflow.
